cards.front: Which Napa Valley AVA is known for producing Cabernet Sauvignon with firm tannins and age-worthiness due to its well-drained volcanic and alluvial soils on the valley floor?
cards.back: Rutherford, known for 'Rutherford dust' — a characteristic grippy tannin quality associated with its benchland soils

cards.front: What climate type does Napa Valley have, and what geographic feature moderates its temperatures?
cards.back: Warm Mediterranean climate; San Pablo Bay funnels cool Pacific air and fog northward through the Carneros region, moderating temperatures

cards.front: Which Napa Valley sub-AVA sits at high elevation on the western mountain range and is known for small-berried, concentrated Cabernet Sauvignon?
cards.back: Howell Mountain

cards.front: What is the coolest AVA shared between Napa and Sonoma, and what varieties thrive there?
cards.back: Los Carneros (Carneros); Chardonnay and Pinot Noir thrive due to cool temperatures and strong winds from San Pablo Bay

cards.front: Which Sonoma AVA is particularly well-regarded for Pinot Noir and Chardonnay, influenced by the Petaluma Wind Gap?
cards.back: Sonoma Coast

cards.front: What is the Russian River Valley known for, and what climatic influence makes it suitable for those varieties?
cards.back: Pinot Noir and Chardonnay; the valley channels cool Pacific fog and air inland, creating a cool climate with a long growing season

cards.front: Which Sonoma AVA is the warmest and best known for Zinfandel and Cabernet Sauvignon?
cards.back: Alexander Valley

cards.front: What soil type is commonly found in the Russian River Valley and is considered ideal for Pinot Noir?
cards.back: Goldridge sandy loam — well-drained, low-fertility soil that stresses the vine and promotes small berries with concentrated flavors

cards.front: Dry Creek Valley in Sonoma is particularly noted for which grape variety?
cards.back: Zinfandel

cards.front: What is Oregon's most important wine region, and what is its dominant grape variety?
cards.back: Willamette Valley; Pinot Noir

cards.front: How does Oregon's climate differ from California's in terms of its effect on Pinot Noir?
cards.back: Oregon has a cooler, wetter maritime climate with a risk of autumn rain at harvest, producing Pinot Noir with higher acidity, lower alcohol, and more restrained fruit compared to California

cards.front: Name two important sub-AVAs within the Willamette Valley known for distinct soil types.
cards.back: Dundee Hills (red Jory volcanic basalt soils) and Ribbon Ridge or Chehalem Mountains (sedimentary soils including Willakenzie)

cards.front: What white variety is considered the second most important in Oregon's Willamette Valley?
cards.back: Pinot Gris

cards.front: What mountain range protects Washington State's main wine regions from Pacific rainfall, creating a semi-arid continental climate?
cards.back: The Cascade Mountains

cards.front: What is Washington State's largest and most important wine AVA?
cards.back: Columbia Valley

cards.front: What are the two leading red grape varieties grown in Washington State's Columbia Valley?
cards.back: Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot

cards.front: How does Washington State's high latitude (around 46–47°N) benefit viticulture despite its continental climate?
cards.back: Long summer daylight hours (up to 17 hours) allow grapes to fully ripen despite the cool nights, which also help retain acidity and freshness

cards.front: Which Washington AVA, located within the Columbia Valley, is known for producing structured Cabernet Sauvignon and Syrah from ancient volcanic basalt soils?
cards.back: Walla Walla Valley

cards.front: What is a key viticultural risk in Washington State's Columbia Valley due to its continental climate?
cards.back: Extreme winter freeze, which can kill vines; some producers use cold-hardy rootstocks or bury vines in winter for protection

cards.front: What is the most widely planted red grape variety in Argentina?
cards.back: Malbec

cards.front: From which French region did Malbec originate before becoming Argentina's signature variety?
cards.back: South West France (Cahors region)

cards.front: Which Argentine province is considered the most important region for high-quality Malbec production?
cards.back: Mendoza

cards.front: What is the sub-region within Mendoza known for producing the highest-quality, most concentrated Malbec at elevations above 900m?
cards.back: Luján de Cuyo

cards.front: Describe the typical flavour profile of Argentine Malbec from Mendoza.
cards.back: Deep colour; plum, black cherry, violet aromas; full body, velvety tannins, good acidity; often oak-aged adding vanilla and spice notes

cards.front: How does high altitude affect Argentine Malbec production in regions like Mendoza?
cards.back: High altitude increases UV radiation and diurnal temperature variation, producing grapes with thick skins (colour/tannin), good acidity, and concentrated flavours

cards.front: Which Argentine region at very high altitudes (up to 3,000m) is an emerging area for Malbec production?
cards.back: Salta (specifically Cafayate/Calchaquí Valleys)

cards.front: What is the dominant red grape variety in Chile's premium wine production?
cards.back: Cabernet Sauvignon

cards.front: Which Chilean valley is most closely associated with high-quality Cabernet Sauvignon production?
cards.back: Maipo Valley

cards.front: Describe the typical flavour profile of Chilean Cabernet Sauvignon from Maipo Valley.
cards.back: Blackcurrant, eucalyptus/mint, cedar; full body, firm tannins, good acidity; often aged in French oak adding spice and vanilla

cards.front: How do the Andes Mountains influence Chilean viticulture?
cards.back: They provide meltwater for irrigation, create a rain shadow effect keeping vineyards dry, and contribute to cool nights increasing diurnal temperature variation

cards.front: Which Chilean sub-appellation within Maipo is particularly renowned for premium Cabernet Sauvignon?
cards.back: Alto Maipo (Puente Alto)

cards.front: What climatic influence from the Pacific Ocean affects Chilean coastal wine regions, moderating temperatures for Cabernet Sauvignon?
cards.back: Cold Humboldt Current brings cold air and coastal fog, cooling vineyards and extending the growing season

cards.front: How does Argentine Malbec differ stylistically from its French counterpart in Cahors?
cards.back: Argentine Malbec is typically riper, fuller-bodied with softer tannins, more fruit-forward with plum and violet notes; Cahors Malbec is more austere, tannic, and earthy

cards.front: What soil type is commonly found in Luján de Cuyo (Mendoza) that benefits Malbec, and what is the irrigation source?
cards.back: Alluvial soils (sandy/gravelly loam); irrigation sourced from Andean snowmelt via canals, as the region is semi-desert with very low rainfall

cards.front: What climate does the Barossa Valley have, and how does it influence Shiraz style?
cards.back: Barossa Valley has a warm to hot continental climate. High temperatures produce full-bodied Shiraz with high alcohol, rich dark fruit (blackberry, plum), chocolate, and often sweet spice from American oak aging.

cards.front: What are the typical flavor characteristics of Barossa Valley Shiraz?
cards.back: Dark fruits (blackberry, plum), chocolate, licorice, sweet spice (vanilla, coconut from American oak), full body, high alcohol, and soft tannins.

cards.front: Why are old vines significant in the Barossa Valley for Shiraz production?
cards.back: Barossa has some of the world's oldest Shiraz vines (some over 150 years old), which survived phylloxera. Old vines produce lower yields with more concentrated flavors, contributing to premium wine quality.

cards.front: What oak aging regime is typically used for Barossa Valley Shiraz, and what flavors does it impart?
cards.back: Traditionally aged in American oak (new and used hogsheads), imparting vanilla, coconut, and sweet spice. Some producers now use French oak for more subtle integration.

cards.front: How does the Eden Valley differ from the Barossa Valley floor in terms of climate and Shiraz style?
cards.back: Eden Valley is higher in altitude, giving a cooler climate. Shiraz from Eden Valley is more elegant with higher acidity, more restrained fruit, and greater finesse compared to the richer Barossa Valley floor style.

cards.front: What is the climate of the Hunter Valley, and why is it considered challenging for viticulture?
cards.back: Hunter Valley has a warm, humid subtropical climate. Harvest-time rainfall and cloud cover are major challenges, increasing disease pressure and making vintage variation significant.

cards.front: What are the key characteristics of Hunter Valley Semillon in its youth?
cards.back: In youth, Hunter Valley Semillon is light-bodied, high in acidity, low in alcohol (around 10-11% ABV), with citrus (lemon, lime) and grassy flavors. It is typically unoaked.

cards.front: How does Hunter Valley Semillon evolve with age?
cards.back: With 5–10+ years of bottle age, Hunter Valley Semillon develops toasty, honeyed, and waxy characteristics with lanolin notes, while retaining its high acidity. It gains complexity without oak influence.

cards.front: Why is Hunter Valley Semillon harvested early, and what effect does this have on the wine?
cards.back: It is harvested early to preserve high natural acidity and keep alcohol low (around 10-11% ABV). This early harvest also retains primary citrus flavors and ensures the wine's aging potential.

cards.front: What winemaking technique is typically used for Hunter Valley Semillon, and why?
cards.back: Hunter Valley Semillon is typically fermented and aged in stainless steel with no oak contact. This preserves its delicate citrus fruit and high acidity, allowing tertiary characters to develop through bottle aging.

cards.front: What is the quality level and aging potential of premium Hunter Valley Semillon?
cards.back: Premium Hunter Valley Semillon is considered one of Australia's most distinctive white wines. It can age for 10–20 years, developing complex toasty, honeyed, and waxy flavors while maintaining freshness from high acidity.

cards.front: What soil types are found in the Barossa Valley, and how do they affect Shiraz?
cards.back: Barossa Valley has a variety of soils including sandy loam, clay, and red-brown earth. Sandy soils in some areas helped vines survive phylloxera. Soils contribute to the richness and concentration of Barossa Shiraz.

cards.front: Which Australian wine region is most famous for Semillon as a single varietal wine?
cards.back: The Hunter Valley in New South Wales is Australia's most famous region for unoaked, age-worthy Semillon as a single varietal wine.

cards.front: How does Barossa Valley Shiraz compare in style to a cool-climate Syrah from the Northern Rhône?
cards.back: Barossa Shiraz is fuller-bodied, higher in alcohol, with richer dark fruit, chocolate, and sweet spice from American oak. Northern Rhône Syrah is more restrained, with savory, peppery notes, higher acidity, and firmer tannins.

cards.front: What is the significance of phylloxera history in the Barossa Valley?
cards.back: The Barossa Valley was largely unaffected by phylloxera, allowing many vines to remain ungrafted on their own rootstocks. This has preserved some of the world's oldest Shiraz vines, which are a key asset for premium wine production.

cards.front: In which region of New Zealand is Marlborough located?
cards.back: Marlborough is located at the northern tip of the South Island of New Zealand.

cards.front: What are the two main sub-regions of Marlborough?
cards.back: The Wairau Valley and the Awatere Valley are the two main sub-regions of Marlborough.

cards.front: What climate type does Marlborough have, and how does it influence Sauvignon Blanc?
cards.back: Marlborough has a cool, sunny, and dry climate with a large diurnal temperature range, which preserves high natural acidity and intense aromatic fruit character in Sauvignon Blanc.

cards.front: What are the typical primary aroma descriptors for Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc?
cards.back: Typical descriptors include green capsicum, asparagus, freshly cut grass, passion fruit, and grapefruit.

cards.front: How is Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc typically vinified, and why?
cards.back: It is typically vinified in stainless steel at cool temperatures with no oak contact, to preserve its fresh, aromatic, and fruit-driven character.

cards.front: What is the typical palate profile of Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc in terms of sweetness, acidity, and body?
cards.back: It is typically dry, with high acidity, light to medium body, and medium to high alcohol.

cards.front: How does the Awatere Valley style of Sauvignon Blanc differ from the Wairau Valley style?
cards.back: The Awatere Valley is cooler and windier, producing wines with higher acidity, more herbaceous and mineral character, and less tropical fruit compared to the riper, more tropical Wairau Valley style.

cards.front: What grape variety dominates Marlborough's plantings, and what percentage of New Zealand's Sauvignon Blanc comes from Marlborough?
cards.back: Sauvignon Blanc dominates Marlborough's plantings, and Marlborough produces the vast majority — approximately 70–75% — of all New Zealand wine, with Sauvignon Blanc being the key variety.

cards.front: What soil types are found in the Wairau Valley and how do they affect Sauvignon Blanc?
cards.back: The Wairau Valley has free-draining alluvial and stony soils that limit vine vigour and water retention, contributing to concentrated flavours and good fruit expression in Sauvignon Blanc.

cards.front: What is the quality and price positioning of Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc in the global market?
cards.back: Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc is predominantly positioned as a good-quality, commercially successful, and accessibly priced wine, widely exported and recognised for its consistent aromatic style.

cards.front: What grape variety is Pinotage, and where was it created?
cards.back: Pinotage is a cross between Pinot Noir and Cinsault, created in South Africa in the 1920s by Abraham Izak Perold.

cards.front: What are the typical flavour characteristics of Pinotage?
cards.back: Red and dark fruits (plum, blackberry), smoky, earthy notes, and sometimes acetone or paint-like aromas if poorly made. Can show coffee and chocolate when oak-aged.

cards.front: What climate does Stellenbosch have, and how does it influence wine style?
cards.back: Stellenbosch has a warm Mediterranean climate, moderated by the Atlantic Ocean and Cape Doctor wind, producing ripe, full-bodied red wines with good structure.

cards.front: Why is Chenin Blanc so significant in South Africa?
cards.back: Chenin Blanc is the most widely planted grape variety in South Africa, used to produce a wide range of styles from dry to sweet and sparkling.

cards.front: What styles of wine can be produced from South African Chenin Blanc?
cards.back: Dry unoaked, dry oaked (wooded), off-dry, late harvest, botrytised sweet wines, and Cap Classique sparkling wines.

cards.front: What is the Cape Doctor and how does it benefit viticulture in Stellenbosch?
cards.back: The Cape Doctor is a strong south-easterly wind that cools vineyards and reduces humidity, lowering the risk of fungal diseases in Stellenbosch.

cards.front: What are the typical tannin and body characteristics of a quality Stellenbosch Pinotage?
cards.back: Full body, high tannins, high alcohol, and high acidity, with the potential for significant ageing when well made.

cards.front: How does old-vine Chenin Blanc differ in style from younger vine examples in South Africa?
cards.back: Old-vine Chenin Blanc (often 35+ years) produces lower yields with greater concentration, complexity, and depth of flavour compared to younger vine examples.

cards.front: What soils are commonly found in Stellenbosch and how do they affect wine quality?
cards.back: Stellenbosch has diverse soils including granite, sandstone, and decomposed granite. These well-drained soils stress the vine, concentrating flavours and contributing to structured, complex wines.

cards.front: What is the Wine of Origin (WO) system and how does it apply to Stellenbosch?
cards.back: The WO system is South Africa's appellation system guaranteeing origin, variety, and vintage. Stellenbosch is a designated WO district within the Coastal Region, known for premium red wines.

cards.front: What is the ideal average growing season temperature range for quality wine production?
cards.back: Between 10°C and 20°C (50°F–68°F). Below 10°C vines struggle to ripen; above 20°C grapes may over-ripen and lose acidity.

cards.front: What is the difference between continental and maritime climates in viticulture?
cards.back: Continental: large diurnal and seasonal temperature variation, cold winters, risk of frost. Maritime: moderated by proximity to water, smaller temperature extremes, higher humidity risk.

cards.front: What is a Mediterranean climate and why is it suited to viticulture?
cards.back: Hot, dry summers and mild, wet winters. Reliable sunshine ensures consistent ripening; low disease pressure due to dry summers.

cards.front: What is the role of diurnal temperature variation in wine quality?
cards.back: Large diurnal range (warm days, cool nights) slows ripening, preserves natural acidity, and helps develop aromatic complexity in grapes.

cards.front: What are the main climate hazards that threaten viticulture?
cards.back: Frost (spring and winter), hail, drought, excessive rain (dilution, disease), and extreme heat (sunburn, over-ripening).

cards.front: How does aspect (slope orientation) affect vine growing conditions?
cards.back: South-facing slopes (in Northern Hemisphere) receive more sunlight and warmth, aiding ripening. Slopes also improve cold air drainage, reducing frost risk.

cards.front: What is the effect of altitude on viticulture?
cards.back: Higher altitude = lower temperatures and increased UV radiation. Helps retain acidity and freshness in warm regions; increases diurnal variation.

cards.front: What are the key soil characteristics important for viticulture?
cards.back: Good drainage, low to moderate fertility, ability to retain some water, and good heat retention. Vines stressed by low fertility produce lower yields with more concentrated flavours.

cards.front: Why is good soil drainage important for vines?
cards.back: Prevents waterlogging, which can suffocate roots and promote disease. Forces roots to grow deep, accessing subsoil water and nutrients, improving vine stability.

cards.front: What is the significance of low soil fertility in viticulture?
cards.back: Low fertility limits vine vigour, reducing excessive canopy growth and directing energy into fruit production, often improving grape concentration and quality.

cards.front: How do clay soils differ from sandy soils in viticulture?
cards.back: Clay: high water retention, slow to warm, can waterlog. Sandy: low water retention, well-drained, warms quickly, low phylloxera risk. Both affect vine stress and wine style.

cards.front: What is the purpose of winter pruning in viticulture?
cards.back: To control the number of buds left on the vine, thereby regulating yield and canopy size for the coming growing season.

cards.front: What is the difference between spur pruning and cane pruning?
cards.back: Spur pruning: short spurs (2–3 buds) left on a permanent framework. Cane pruning: longer canes (8–12 buds) replaced each year. Cane pruning suits varieties with unfruitful basal buds.

cards.front: What is Guyot training and where is it commonly used?
cards.back: A cane-pruned system with one (single Guyot) or two (double Guyot) canes tied horizontally. Widely used in Bordeaux, Burgundy, and many cool-climate regions.

cards.front: What is the Gobelet (bush vine) training system and what are its advantages?
cards.back: Free-standing, head-trained vines with no trellis. Low to ground, provides shade to grapes. Suited to hot, dry, windy climates. Low cost but difficult to mechanise.

cards.front: What is the purpose of a trellis system in viticulture?
cards.back: Supports the vine, lifts the canopy for sunlight and airflow, facilitates mechanisation, and can be adjusted to manage vigour and microclimate.

cards.front: What is the VSP (Vertical Shoot Positioning) training system?
cards.back: Shoots are trained vertically upward between foliage wires. Creates an open, well-exposed canopy. Widely used in cool and moderate climates; suits mechanisation.

cards.front: What is canopy management and why is it important?
cards.back: Practices (leaf removal, shoot thinning, hedging) to optimise sunlight and airflow within the canopy. Improves grape ripeness, reduces disease, and enhances wine quality.

cards.front: How does the proximity of large bodies of water moderate vineyard climate?
cards.back: Water absorbs and releases heat slowly, reducing temperature extremes. This moderates summer heat and winter cold, extending the growing season and reducing frost risk.

cards.front: What is the relationship between vine vigour and wine quality?
cards.back: High vigour (lush canopy growth) shades fruit, delays ripening, and dilutes flavour. Low to moderate vigour, often from poor soils or water stress, concentrates flavours and improves quality.

cards.front: What are the two types of frost that pose a hazard to vines?
cards.back: Winter frost (damages dormant wood and buds at around -20°C) and spring frost (damages newly emerged green shoots after budbreak).

cards.front: What are three methods used to protect vines from spring frost?
cards.back: Wind machines (mix warmer air above with cold air at ground level), heaters/smudge pots (raise air temperature), and sprinkler systems (water freezes around buds releasing latent heat, protecting tissue).

cards.front: Why are valley floors more susceptible to spring frost than hillside sites?
cards.back: Cold air is denser than warm air and flows downhill, pooling in valleys and low-lying areas, creating frost pockets.

cards.front: How does phylloxera damage Vitis vinifera vines?
cards.back: The root-feeding form of the louse feeds on the roots of V. vinifera, causing lesions that allow fungal infection, rotting the roots and eventually killing the vine.

cards.front: What is the primary method used to combat phylloxera in vineyards today?
cards.back: Grafting V. vinifera scions onto phylloxera-resistant American vine rootstocks, which tolerate root feeding without suffering fatal damage.

cards.front: Why are vines grown in sandy soils less affected by phylloxera?
cards.back: Phylloxera cannot easily move through sandy soils, so the louse is unable to spread effectively and reach vine roots.

cards.front: What conditions favour the development of powdery mildew (Oidium), and how is it controlled?
cards.back: Powdery mildew thrives in warm, dry conditions with high humidity; it does not require free water. It is controlled by sulfur-based sprays.

cards.front: What conditions favour downy mildew, and what is used to control it?
cards.back: Downy mildew requires warm, wet, humid conditions with free water on leaf surfaces. It is controlled with copper-based sprays (e.g., Bordeaux mixture).

cards.front: What is Botrytis cinerea, and under what two contrasting conditions can it affect grapes?
cards.back: Botrytis cinerea is a fungal disease. In wet, humid conditions it causes grey rot (damaging grapes). In warm days and misty mornings with dry afternoons it causes noble rot (botrytis bunch rot), which concentrates sugars and adds complexity, desirable for sweet wine production.

cards.front: Name two regions where phylloxera has had limited impact due to soil type or isolation, allowing ungrafted vines to survive.
cards.back: Chile (isolated by natural barriers — Andes, Pacific, desert, and Antarctic) and parts of South Australia with sandy soils (e.g., some areas of the Barossa Valley).

cards.front: What is the primary purpose of alcoholic fermentation in winemaking?
cards.back: Yeast converts sugars (glucose and fructose) into alcohol (ethanol) and CO2, transforming grape juice into wine.

cards.front: What is the ideal temperature range for white wine fermentation and why?
cards.back: 12–18°C. Lower temperatures preserve delicate fruit aromas and freshness by slowing fermentation and retaining volatile aromatics.

cards.front: What is the ideal temperature range for red wine fermentation?
cards.back: 20–32°C. Higher temperatures promote extraction of colour, tannins, and flavour compounds from grape skins.

cards.front: What is malolactic fermentation (MLF)?
cards.back: A bacterial process where lactic acid bacteria (LAB) convert sharp malic acid into softer lactic acid and CO2, reducing acidity and adding complexity.

cards.front: What flavour compound is produced during MLF and what aroma does it contribute?
cards.back: Diacetyl, which contributes a buttery or creamy aroma to wine.

cards.front: Why might a winemaker choose to prevent MLF in a white wine?
cards.back: To retain high natural acidity and fresh, crisp fruit character — desirable in wines like Riesling or Sauvignon Blanc from cool climates.

cards.front: Name three ways a winemaker can prevent MLF from occurring.
cards.back: 1. Adding SO2 to inhibit bacteria. 2. Sterile filtration to remove bacteria. 3. Keeping wine at low temperatures to inhibit bacterial activity.

cards.front: What are the two main roles of sulfur dioxide (SO2) in winemaking?
cards.back: 1. Antioxidant — protects wine from oxidation. 2. Antimicrobial — inhibits unwanted yeasts and bacteria.

cards.front: What is the difference between free SO2 and bound SO2?
cards.back: Free SO2 is the active, protective form available in wine. Bound SO2 has combined with other wine compounds (e.g. aldehydes) and is no longer protective. Total SO2 = free + bound.

cards.front: At what pH is SO2 most effective as an antimicrobial agent?
cards.back: SO2 is most effective at lower pH (higher acidity). At low pH, a greater proportion exists as molecular SO2, which is the most antimicrobial form.

cards.front: What are the main flavour characteristics MLF adds to red wines?
cards.back: Reduced sharp acidity, increased softness and roundness, added complexity, and creamy/buttery notes from diacetyl.

cards.front: What are the three main types of oak vessel used in winemaking?
cards.back: 1. Small oak barrels (barriques, ~225L). 2. Large oak casks/foudres. 3. Oak alternatives (staves, chips, inner staves).

cards.front: How does barrel size affect the rate of oak flavour extraction?
cards.back: Smaller barrels have a higher surface-area-to-volume ratio, resulting in faster and greater oak flavour extraction compared to larger vessels.

cards.front: What flavour compounds does oak impart to wine?
cards.back: Vanilla (vanillin), toast, smoke, spice (clove, nutmeg), coconut (from American oak lactones), and cedar/sandalwood notes.

cards.front: How does the level of oak toasting affect the flavours imparted to wine?
cards.back: Light toast: more wood tannins, vanilla. Medium toast: balanced vanilla, spice, toast. Heavy toast: more smoke, coffee, bitter chocolate; fewer wood tannins.

cards.front: What is the difference between French oak and American oak in terms of flavour contribution?
cards.back: French oak: subtler, more spice, cedar, and fine tannins. American oak: more pronounced coconut, vanilla, and dill character due to higher lactone content.

cards.front: How does oak ageing affect a wine's structure beyond flavour?
cards.back: Oak allows slow ingress of oxygen (micro-oxygenation), softening tannins, stabilising colour, and adding texture and complexity.

cards.front: Why does a new oak barrel impart more flavour than an older one?
cards.back: With each use, soluble flavour compounds are extracted, so older barrels contribute progressively less oak flavour. After 3–4 uses, barrels mainly provide oxygen exposure.

cards.front: What is the role of SO2 added at harvest/crushing?
cards.back: To protect must from oxidation and inhibit wild/spoilage yeasts and bacteria before and during fermentation, giving cultured yeast a competitive advantage.

cards.front: What conditions favour the completion of MLF?
cards.back: Warm temperatures (18–22°C), low SO2 levels, moderate alcohol, higher pH (lower acidity), and the presence of lactic acid bacteria (e.g. Oenococcus oeni).

cards.front: What is the key difference between the Traditional Method and the Charmat Method for producing sparkling wine?
cards.back: In the Traditional Method, the second fermentation occurs in the individual bottle. In the Charmat Method, the second fermentation occurs in a large pressurised tank (autoclave).

cards.front: What is another name for the Charmat Method?
cards.back: The Tank Method (also known as Cuve Close or Metodo Italiano).

cards.front: In the Traditional Method, what is added to the base wine to trigger the second fermentation in bottle?
cards.back: A liqueur de tirage — a mixture of wine, sugar, and yeast.

cards.front: What is riddling (remuage) in the Traditional Method, and why is it not required in the Charmat Method?
cards.back: Riddling is the gradual tilting and rotating of bottles to move yeast sediment into the neck. It is not needed in Charmat because the wine is filtered under pressure in a tank before bottling.

cards.front: What is disgorgement in the Traditional Method, and what is the equivalent step in the Charmat Method?
cards.back: Disgorgement is the removal of yeast sediment from the bottle neck after riddling. In Charmat, the wine is filtered under pressure in the tank — there is no disgorgement step.

cards.front: What is the minimum lees contact time required for Non-Vintage Champagne in the Traditional Method?
cards.back: A minimum of 15 months on the lees (at least 12 months after disgorgement).

cards.front: Why does the Charmat Method generally produce wines with less autolytic (bready, biscuity) character than the Traditional Method?
cards.back: Charmat wines typically spend much less time in contact with the yeast lees, so there is less autolysis and therefore less development of bready, biscuity, or toasty flavours.

cards.front: Name two sparkling wines typically produced using the Charmat Method and one produced using the Traditional Method.
cards.back: Charmat Method: Prosecco (Italy) and Asti Spumante (Italy). Traditional Method: Champagne (France).

cards.front: Why is the Charmat Method considered more suitable for aromatic grape varieties such as Glera (used in Prosecco)?
cards.back: The shorter production process and less time on lees preserves the fresh, primary fruit and floral aromas of aromatic varieties, which would be masked by autolytic characters developed in the Traditional Method.

cards.front: What is the dosage (liqueur d'expédition) and at what stage is it added in both the Traditional Method and the Charmat Method?
cards.back: The dosage is a mixture of wine and sugar added to adjust the final sweetness level. In the Traditional Method it is added after disgorgement; in the Charmat Method it is added to the wine in the tank before bottling under pressure.

cards.front: What does SAT stand for in WSET Level 3 systematic approach to tasting?
cards.back: Systematic Approach to Tasting — a structured method covering Appearance, Nose, and Palate to assess style, quality, and readiness for drinking.

cards.front: What are the five quality levels used in the WSET L3 SAT conclusion?
cards.back: Faulty, Poor, Acceptable, Good, Very Good, Outstanding. (Six levels total including Faulty.)

cards.front: What does 'balance' mean in the context of WSET L3 quality assessment?
cards.back: Balance refers to the harmonious relationship between acidity, tannin, alcohol, sweetness, and fruit. No single element dominates negatively.

cards.front: How does 'length' contribute to quality assessment in the SAT?
cards.back: Length (finish) is the time flavours persist after swallowing. Longer finish generally indicates higher quality; a short finish suggests lower quality.

cards.front: What is 'intensity' in the SAT and how does it relate to quality?
cards.back: Intensity refers to the concentration of aromas and flavours. Higher intensity can indicate higher quality, but must be considered alongside complexity and balance.

cards.front: What does 'complexity' mean in WSET L3 quality assessment?
cards.back: Complexity refers to the range and layers of different aromas and flavours (primary, secondary, tertiary). Greater complexity is associated with higher quality wines.

cards.front: What are the three categories of aromas/flavours assessed on the nose and palate in the SAT?
cards.back: Primary (fruit, floral, herbaceous — from grape), Secondary (from winemaking, e.g. yeast autolysis, MLF), and Tertiary (from ageing in oak or bottle).

cards.front: What is the difference between 'typicity' and quality in the WSET L3 SAT?
cards.back: Typicity is how well a wine reflects the expected characteristics of its grape variety, region, or style. It informs quality assessment but a wine can be high quality without strict typicity.

cards.front: How does tannin level and quality affect the overall quality conclusion in a red wine?
cards.back: Tannins should be assessed for level (low–high) and texture (harsh/grippy to silky/ripe). Ripe, well-integrated tannins contribute positively to balance and quality.

cards.front: What role does acidity play in assessing quality using the SAT?
cards.back: Acidity provides freshness and structure. Appropriate acidity for the style contributes to balance and length; too little makes wine flabby, too much makes it harsh.

cards.front: What is a 'faulty' wine in the WSET L3 SAT quality scale and give two examples of faults?
cards.back: A faulty wine has a defect that makes it unpleasant or unacceptable. Examples: cork taint (TCA — musty/damp cardboard) and oxidation (flat, stale, nutty aromas in a wine not intended to be oxidative).

cards.front: What does 'readiness for drinking / potential for ageing' assess in the SAT conclusion?
cards.back: It evaluates whether a wine is too young, at peak, or past its best, based on fruit freshness, tannin/acid levels, tertiary character development, and overall balance.

cards.front: How is alcohol level described in the WSET L3 SAT and what are the three broad categories?
cards.back: Alcohol is described as Low (below 11% ABV), Medium (11–13.9% ABV), and High (14% ABV and above). It contributes to body and balance.

cards.front: What is 'body' in the SAT and what factors contribute to it?
cards.back: Body is the weight and fullness of wine in the mouth. It is influenced by alcohol, residual sugar, extract, and tannin. Described as light, medium, or full.

cards.front: Why is it important to assess all SAT components together rather than individually when concluding on quality?
cards.back: Quality is a holistic judgement. A wine may score highly on intensity but lack balance or length. All factors — balance, intensity, complexity, length, and typicity — must be weighed together for an accurate quality conclusion.

cards.front: What are the key characteristics of Riesling grape and wine style?
cards.back: Thin-skinned, early budding, late ripening. High acidity retained even at full ripeness. Aromas: green apple, citrus, peach, petrol (with age). Low to high alcohol. Made dry to sweet. Does not typically use oak.

cards.front: What climates suit Pinot Noir and why is it considered difficult to grow?
cards.back: Cool to moderate climates (Burgundy, Champagne, Oregon, NZ Marlborough). Thin-skinned — susceptible to rot, frost, coulure. Buds early (frost risk). Needs careful site selection. Produces light-bodied, high-acid, low-to-medium tannin reds with red fruit and earthy notes.

cards.front: Describe the typical flavor profile and structure of Cabernet Sauvignon.
cards.back: Full body, high tannins, high acidity, high alcohol. Aromas: blackcurrant, black cherry, cedar, tobacco, mint. Thick-skinned, late ripening. Needs warm climates to fully ripen. Benefits from oak aging and blending (e.g., Merlot, Cabernet Franc).

cards.front: What distinguishes Merlot from Cabernet Sauvignon in terms of structure and flavor?
cards.back: Merlot: medium-to-full body, lower tannins, lower acidity than Cab Sauv, higher alcohol potential. Flavors: plum, black cherry, chocolate, fruitcake. Ripens earlier, thinner skin. More approachable young. Key grape in Pomerol and St-Émilion.

cards.front: What are the viticultural challenges and wine characteristics of Gewurztraminer?
cards.back: Pink-skinned, low acidity, high alcohol, deep color for a white. Highly aromatic: lychee, rose petal, ginger, Turkish delight. Prone to low yields and oxidation. Best in Alsace, Alto Adige, Germany. Made dry to sweet (Vendange Tardive, SGN).

cards.front: How does Syrah/Shiraz differ in style between cool and warm climates?
cards.back: Cool climate (N. Rhône, cool Aus): medium-full body, high tannins, high acidity, black pepper, violet, olive, savory notes. Warm climate (Barossa): full body, ripe blackberry, plum, chocolate, eucalyptus, lower acidity, more jammy. Same grape, different expression.

cards.front: What are the key characteristics of Chardonnay that make it so versatile?
cards.back: Neutral grape — reflects terroir and winemaking. Can be unoaked (crisp, green apple, citrus) or oaked (butter, vanilla, toast, tropical fruit). High to medium acidity. Suits cool (Chablis) to warm (Napa) climates. Used in still, sparkling, and sweet wines.

cards.front: Describe the characteristics of Sauvignon Blanc across different climates.
cards.back: High acidity, dry, light-to-medium body. Cool climate (Loire, Marlborough): grassy, green pepper, gooseberry, passion fruit, herbaceous. Warm climate: tropical fruit, less herbaceous. Rarely oaked (except Bordeaux Blanc blends). Rarely aged long.

cards.front: What are the defining characteristics of Nebbiolo?
cards.back: High tannins, high acidity, high alcohol, pale garnet color. Aromas: tar, roses, cherry, leather, tobacco, dried herbs. Late ripening — needs warm sites. Key in Barolo and Barbaresco (Piedmont). Requires long aging to soften tannins.

cards.front: What makes Grenache/Garnacha distinctive as a grape variety?
cards.back: Thin-skinned, low tannins, low acidity, high alcohol, pale color. Aromas: red fruit (strawberry, raspberry), white pepper, herbs. Needs hot, dry, windy conditions (S. Rhône, Priorat, Navarra). Often blended (GSM). Used in rosé and fortified wines (Banyuls).

cards.front: Describe the key characteristics of Pinot Gris/Pinot Grigio and how style varies by region.
cards.back: Mutation of Pinot Noir — pink/grey skin. Alsace (Pinot Gris): full body, low acidity, rich, spicy, stone fruit, can be sweet. Italy (Pinot Grigio): light body, high acidity, neutral, crisp, pear, apple. Same grape, very different styles based on yield and winemaking.

cards.front: What are the characteristics of Tempranillo and its key growing regions?
cards.back: Medium-to-full body, medium-high tannins, medium acidity, low alcohol potential. Aromas: cherry, plum, leather, tobacco, vanilla (oak). Thin-skinned, early ripening. Key regions: Rioja, Ribera del Duero, Douro (Tinta Roriz). Often aged in American oak.

cards.front: What distinguishes Viognier from other white varieties in terms of aroma and structure?
cards.back: Low acidity, high alcohol, full body, deep golden color. Highly aromatic: peach, apricot, blossom, violet, musk. Difficult to grow — low yields, prone to powdery mildew. Key in Condrieu (N. Rhône). Sometimes co-fermented with Syrah (Côte-Rôtie).

cards.front: What are the characteristics of Sangiovese and how do they vary by clone and region?
cards.back: High acidity, high tannins, medium-to-full body, medium alcohol. Aromas: cherry, plum, dried herbs, leather, tobacco. Thin-skinned, late ripening. Chianti Classico (lighter, more acidic) vs. Brunello di Montalcino (Sangiovese Grosso — fuller, more structured, longer aging).

cards.front: Describe the characteristics of Chenin Blanc and the range of styles it produces.
cards.back: High acidity retained even at full ripeness. Aromas: green apple, quince, honey, beeswax, lanolin. Versatile: dry (Savennières), off-dry, sparkling (Crémant de Loire, Vouvray), botrytized sweet (Quarts de Chaume, Bonnezeaux). Ages exceptionally well. Also important in South Africa.

cards.front: What are the key characteristics of Malbec and how has its style evolved in Argentina?
cards.back: Deep color, full body, medium-high tannins, medium acidity. Aromas: blackberry, plum, violet, chocolate, leather. Originally from Cahors (France) — rustic, tannic. In Mendoza (Argentina): riper, softer tannins, more fruit-forward, velvety texture. High altitude sites add freshness.

cards.front: What are the characteristics of Muscat Blanc à Petits Grains and what styles does it produce?
cards.back: Considered the finest Muscat variety. Aromas: fresh grape, orange blossom, rose, peach — grapey and floral. Made dry (Alsace), sparkling (Asti DOCG — low alcohol, sweet), fortified (Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise, Australian Liqueur Muscat). Consistent aromatic character across all styles.

cards.front: How does Cabernet Franc differ from Cabernet Sauvignon in structure and flavor?
cards.back: Lighter body, lower tannins, lower alcohol, higher acidity than Cab Sauv. Aromas: red fruit (raspberry, strawberry), violet, graphite, green pepper (if underripe), tobacco. Ripens earlier. Key in Loire (Chinon, Bourgueil) as varietal; blending grape in Bordeaux. More elegant, less tannic.

cards.front: What are the characteristics of Zinfandel/Primitivo and what styles does it produce?
cards.back: Thin-skinned, uneven ripening (raisined and unripe berries simultaneously). High alcohol potential, medium tannins, medium acidity. Aromas: blackberry, raspberry jam, black pepper, spice. Styles: dry red (Napa, Sonoma), White Zinfandel (off-dry rosé), late harvest. Same as Primitivo (Puglia, Italy).

cards.front: What are the key characteristics of Grüner Veltliner?
cards.back: Austria's flagship white grape. High acidity, dry, light-to-full body depending on style. Distinctive white pepper note, green herbs, citrus, stone fruit. Lighter styles (Steinfeder): crisp, early drinking. Richer styles (Smaragd, Federspiel): full body, aging potential. Mainly grown in Wachau, Kamptal, Kremstal.
